authoritative_sources.title: AS-11: Media Contribution File Formats
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AMWA's official specifications page defines AS-11 as constrained media file formats for delivery of finished media assets to a broadcaster or publisher, making it authoritative for broadcaster-facing delivery-package terminology and boundaries.
used_for: distinction between finished media assets and generic files
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Constrains the lane to ask for the governing delivery profile and finished-asset package context before treating a request as ordinary distribution work.

authoritative_sources.title: AS-11 X9
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The published AS-11 X9 specification is an authoritative AMWA rendering for North American broadcaster delivery and explicitly frames the package as vendor-neutral, broadcaster-neutral, self-contained, and play-out ready.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: North American broadcaster package validation and closeout
used_for: language, audio, and caption track awareness
self-contained package and closeout evidence posture
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Prevents the lane from marking broadcaster fulfillment complete unless the self-contained delivery package and required track evidence line up with the requested profile.

authoritative_sources.title: MovieLabs Digital Distribution Framework
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: MovieLabs' official MDDF overview is authoritative industry guidance for digital movie and television distribution workflows spanning identification, metadata, avails, asset delivery, delivery process support, and reporting.
used_for: identification, metadata, avails, and delivery terminology
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Shapes the pack around actual media supply-chain records instead of generic logistics abstractions and keeps title, metadata, asset delivery, and reporting linked.

authoritative_sources.title: Asset Ordering, Delivery and Tracking
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: MovieLabs AOD is an authoritative industry workflow reference for asset availability, asset orders, content delivery requirements, asset status manifests, QC failure reports, and status or error communication between studios, platforms, and service providers.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Asset planning, delivery execution, and status handling
used_for: asset orders and asset status manifests
QC failure reports and delivery-status handling
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Drives the lane's expected artifacts, systems, and exception posture for planning what assets should be delivered and how status or errors are recorded.

authoritative_sources.title: Avails and Title List
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: MovieLabs Avails and Title List guidance is authoritative for precise licensing and title-availability metadata that constrains which asset orders and fulfillment activities are actually in scope.
used_for: boundary posture between fulfillment and rights approval
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Keeps the lane from treating asset delivery as approved when the controlling title-list or avails context is missing or routed to rights owners.

authoritative_sources.title: Media Delivery Core (MDC)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: MovieLabs MDC is the recognized practical-use guide for Asset Ordering, Delivery and Tracking and provides delivery use cases and examples directly tied to media-distribution execution.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Practical delivery execution and handoff handling
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Turns AOD structure into concrete execution stages for package readiness, transfer monitoring, and completion evidence instead of leaving the workflow abstract.

authoritative_sources.title: How to Use EIDR to Identify Versions for Distribution Purposes FAQ
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: EIDR is the official identifier registry for audiovisual works, and its distribution-purpose FAQ is authoritative for when edits versus manifestations are required for territories, languages, subtitles, and distributor-specific releases.
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Prevents versioning mistakes by requiring the lane to keep release packages tied to the correct title, edit, and manifestation structure rather than free-text version labels.

authoritative_sources.title: SMPTE and the IMF User Group Have Joined Forces
authoritative_sources.publisher: Society of Motion Picture and Television Engineers
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: SMPTE is the standards body behind ST 2067, and its official IMF guidance describes IMF as a B2B package used for versioning and exchange between content owners, post facilities, and distribution platforms.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Versioned package exchange across post and distribution systems
used_for: IMF package role in multi-territory versioning
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Anchors the pack's IMF-specific handling so the lane expects actual package records when streaming or multi-platform fulfillment depends on IMF workflows.

authoritative_sources.title: Quality Control (QC) Vocabulary
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: MovieLabs' QC vocabulary is a recognized control reference for encoding and naming issues found during media QC, which materially constrains how distribution operations interpret failure evidence and exception routing.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: QC evidence interpretation and exception handling
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Keeps QC failure evidence explicit and prevents the lane from closing or reclassifying technical defects without handing them to the correct owner.

authoritative_sources.title: Order on Review and Notice of Apparent Liability for Forfeiture
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The FCC is the U.S. regulator for IP-delivered video captioning requirements, and this order restates distributor obligations to pass through required captions, preserve caption quality, and maintain complaint-handling mechanisms.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Caption pass-through, quality, and distributor-duty handling
used_for: hard-stop conditions when required caption evidence is incomplete
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Adds a regulator-backed refusal trigger when a requester tries to treat missing caption handling as an ordinary file-ops issue.

authoritative_sources.title: SMPTE Timed Text
authoritative_sources.publisher: Society of Motion Picture and Television Engineers
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: SMPTE's official standards index states that SMPTE Timed Text is an FCC safe-harbor interchange and delivery format for online video captioning, making it authoritative for timed-text evidence and format-boundary handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Timed-text format evidence and caption-package validation
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Prevents the lane from improvising caption-delivery format answers when the request actually depends on authoritative timed-text standards or regulator-safe-harbor handling.

workflow_stages.name: title and version grounding
workflow_stages.description: Confirm the title, asset type, target platform or territory, governing edit or manifestation, current lifecycle state, and controlling avails or asset-order context before work advances.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: package, localization, and inventory readiness validation
workflow_stages.description: Verify the required package profile, asset inventory, localization set, timed-text files, metadata, and controlling content delivery requirements before the item moves to ready or in-progress.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: delivery execution and status monitoring
workflow_stages.description: Track asset-order fulfillment, transfer or portal submission state, asset status manifests, vendor dependencies, and partner acknowledgements while preserving version and package traceability.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: exception handling and rework routing
workflow_stages.description: Move work into blocked or rework status when identifiers, manifests, localization assets, QC evidence, captions, or partner feedback conflict and route the issue to the correct owner without overstepping authority.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: completion evidence capture and fulfillment closeout
workflow_stages.description: Assemble partner receipts, asset status updates, transfer evidence, package confirmations, and next-owner notes before marking work completed or handed off.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: avails, title lists, rights window extracts, asset confirmations, asset orders, and content delivery requirements
title, edit, manifestation, and other EIDR-linked identifier records
IMF package records such as CPL, PKL, composition maps, essence references, sidecar manifests, or AS-11 delivery files and checksums
caption, subtitle, dubbed audio, forced narrative, artwork, poster, synopsis, metadata, and timed-text inventory records
QC failure reports, validation results, exception tickets, transfer logs, asset status manifests, and partner portal acknowledgements
completion records, blocked-work notes, availability or logistics summaries, and shortage or vendor-risk escalation notes
systems_of_record: media asset management or digital asset management platform
title metadata and EIDR-linked identifier system
content supply-chain or fulfillment orchestration system
localization and accessibility asset management system
partner delivery portal, traffic handoff tracker, or broadcaster delivery queue
secure file-transfer, object-storage, or checksum-audit system

allowed: Classify media-distribution work as accepted, ready, in-progress, blocked, rework, or completed based on named title, version, package, localization, and delivery evidence.

Prepare evidence-backed readiness summaries, completion records, exception notes, availability or logistics summaries, and escalation packets without claiming rights, engineering, QC, accessibility, or schedule-signoff authority.
Require the minimum tenant facts, source records, and system retrievals needed to continue safely.
escalate: Uncertain avails, rights, or territory records would change the delivery outcome.
Technical conformance failures, QC defects, security requirements, or package-profile conflicts require engineering or specialist review.
Caption, subtitle, or timed-text obligations are missing, conflicting, or outside the lane's authority to waive.
The governing title-management, package-inventory, localization, QC, or partner-receipt records disagree on whether work is actually ready or complete.
refuse: Approve rights sufficiency, contractual availability, legal clearance, privacy interpretation, or other legal judgments.
Approve final QC exceptions, engineering conformance, caption waivers, schedule changes, or traffic go-live commitments.
Treat the industry name as cosmetic and answer without the media-specific title, version, avails, manifest, localization, QC, or partner-delivery evidence.

domain_failure_modes: Work is marked complete even though the partner receipt, asset status manifest, checksum trail, package confirmation, or localization evidence does not materially support closure.
A generic file-ops response ignores media-distribution records such as avails, title lists, EIDR version structure, asset orders, timed-text assets, or QC failure reports.
The wrong title version, language set, or delivery profile is fulfilled because identifiers, manifests, and localization inventory are not kept aligned.
Caption, subtitle, or accessibility obligations are treated as optional operational details instead of answer-changing compliance evidence.
The lane drifts into rights approval, media engineering, QC adjudication, accessibility waiver, legal interpretation, or scheduling authority.
Broadcast, film, and streaming fulfillment paths are flattened into one generic process even though their packages, partner evidence, and completion signals differ materially.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Partner-specific delivery profiles and completion receipts vary materially beyond the public AMWA and MovieLabs baseline.
unresolved_ambiguities.resolution_path: Require tenant retrieval of the active broadcaster, platform, syndication, or aggregator delivery profile and the completion signal that counts as authoritative for that destination.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Territory-specific accessibility and rights-handling obligations vary beyond the reviewed FCC and standards baseline.
unresolved_ambiguities.resolution_path: Require tenant retrieval of governing jurisdiction, platform policy, and owner-routing rules before applying caption, subtitle, or entitlement decisions outside the reviewed baseline.

notes: Authoritative title, version, package, localization, QC, and delivery truth comes from tenant-named media asset, avails, fulfillment, localization, and partner-delivery systems constrained by the public AMWA, MovieLabs, EIDR, SMPTE, and FCC baseline.
The lane stops before rights approval, media engineering, QC signoff, accessibility waivers, scheduling authority, and legal interpretation.
